20 posts with the tag “BMAD”

BMad v6.0.4 + GDS v0.1.10: Edge Case Hunter, Multi-Agent Testing, and Engine Knowledge

Mar 2, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Your Code Review Has a Blind Spot

Section titled “Your Code Review Has a Blind Spot”

Adversarial code review is good at catching design flaws, naming issues, and logic errors. But it has a gap. Branching paths with nested conditions - the ones where input values sit right at a boundary - tend to slip through. You know the kind: what happens when the array is empty? When the counter hits exactly zero? When the user passes null where a string was expected?

BMAD v6.0.4 and GDS v0.1.10 shipped together. Here’s what changed and why it matters.

Edge Case Hunter

Section titled “Edge Case Hunter”

The new Edge Case Hunter is a method-driven review tool. It traces every branching path in your code and checks boundary conditions systematically. It reports only unhandled gaps - no noise, no generic suggestions.

It works alongside adversarial review, not instead of it. Adversarial review catches structural and design problems. Edge Case Hunter catches execution path problems.

Here’s what happened during development: the first version forced the tool to always report findings. If it found nothing, it would halt and retry. The result? The AI fabricated issues. When you pressure an LLM to always produce output, it will produce output - even if there’s nothing to report.

The fix was to remove the zero-findings halt condition (#1797). Let the tool say “I traced all paths, nothing is missing.” That single change made the tool trustworthy. False positives destroy confidence faster than missed bugs.

Test Architect Enhancements

Section titled “Test Architect Enhancements”

Test Architect got four upgrades:

Agent Teams - Coordinate multiple testing agents working in parallel. One agent handles unit tests, another runs integration tests, a third checks API contracts. They share context and avoid duplicating work.

Sub-agents - Complex test scenarios that would overwhelm a single agent can be decomposed. A parent agent defines the test plan, sub-agents execute individual test suites.

Configurable tool modes - Set the testing approach at input. Choose between strict TDD, exploratory testing, or regression-focused modes before the first test runs.

Pact.io contract testing - Service-to-service contracts built into the BMAD workflow. Define what your API promises, verify that consumers and providers agree, catch breaking changes before they reach production.

GDS v0.1.10: Engine Knowledge

Section titled “GDS v0.1.10: Engine Knowledge”

Game Dev Studio now knows your engine. Knowledge bases were added for Unity, Unreal, Godot, and Phaser. Each one covers:

  • Performance optimization patterns specific to that engine
  • Coding conventions and best practices
  • Popular and useful third-party plugins
  • Licensing and cost details
  • Platform-specific caveats
  • When NOT to use this engine (honest guidance)

The game architecture workflow auto-loads the relevant knowledge fragment when you select your engine in Step 3. Steps 4 (Decisions), 6 (Structure), and 7 (Patterns) all use engine-specific context. No more generic “consider performance” advice - the system knows that Unity’s garbage collector has different constraints than Godot’s scene tree.

Credit to @sjennings for this contribution.

Brainstorming Fix

Section titled “Brainstorming Fix”

Previous behavior: start a new brainstorming session and your existing one gets overwritten. All that context, gone.

New behavior: the system detects an existing session and prompts you to continue it or start fresh. Small change, but anyone who lost brainstorming context will appreciate it.

Other Fixes

Section titled “Other Fixes”
  • Installer templates (#1769): Legacy @ path prefixes replaced with explicit {project-root} syntax. More predictable, easier to debug.
  • Docs domain (#1777): Broken documentation domain references in README and issue templates are fixed.

The Numbers

Section titled “The Numbers”

5 commits, 3 PRs, 4 contributors: @lum (2 PRs), @alex, @chrischen, @BMad. Community-driven, solving problems people actually hit.

v6.0.3 Highlights (If You Missed It)

Section titled “v6.0.3 Highlights (If You Missed It)”
  • Root Cause Analysis skill (#1741)
  • Fixed nested directory installer (#1735)
  • OpenCode mode: all support (#1764)
  • CSV manifest quotes fixed (#1746)

Install

Section titled “Install”

If you already have BMAD installed, the installer detects your setup and offers a quick update path.

Links

Section titled “Links”
  • GitHub Releases
  • BMAD-METHOD Repository
  • BMadCode YouTube
  • Support the Project
  • GDS Module Repository

BMAD Builder is coming next.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Development
  • Code Review
  • Testing
  • Game Development

BMAD v6.0.4: Two Minutes from Beta to Stable

Mar 2, 2026
Vibe Sparking

One Command, Two Minutes

Section titled “One Command, Two Minutes”

You type bunx bmad-method install. The installer sees your beta 8 setup and offers a Quick Update. Two minutes later, you have a stable platform with 82 workflows, 27 agents, and configurations for 28 AI tools. Your existing settings are untouched.

That’s the whole upgrade story. But the details are worth knowing.

What Changed: Beta 8 to v6.0.4

Section titled “What Changed: Beta 8 to v6.0.4”

BMAD v6.0.4 is the first official stable release. The “beta” label is gone.

The core and all five modules (bmm, bmb, cis, gds, tea) got updates. The TEA module is the big addition - it brings automated testing into the BMAD ecosystem. The tool count jumped from 18 to 28, covering nearly every AI coding assistant on the market.

Here’s what’s new:

WhatBefore (Beta 8)After (v6.0.4)
Release statusBetaStable
Modules56 (TEA added)
Workflows~7482
AI tool integrations1828
Contract testing-Pact.js + SmartBear MCP
Browser testing-Playwright CLI + MCP
Stack detectionManualAuto-detect
CI platform detectionManualAuto-detect

The Upgrade Process

Section titled “The Upgrade Process”

If you already have BMAD installed, run:

The installer detects your existing installation:

Pick Quick Update. That’s the key decision.

The installer checks each module. In my case, all five existing modules were already structurally up to date - the update applied configuration refinements and the new TEA module setup.

TEA Module: Testing Built In

Section titled “TEA Module: Testing Built In”

The TEA (Test Architect) module is the standout addition. During upgrade, it asks a few questions:

Pact.js contract testing - If you work with microservices, enable this. It integrates @seontechnologies/pactjs-utils for consumer-driven contract testing, with both local monorepo and remote broker workflows.

SmartBear MCP - Connects to PactFlow for broker queries. Requires PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL and PACT_BROKER_TOKEN as env vars.

Execution mode - TEA can orchestrate multi-step generation using agent-teams, subagents, or sequential fallback. The “Auto” option probes your runtime and picks the best mode.

Stack auto-detection - TEA reads your project files to determine if you’re running frontend, backend, or full-stack:

  • Frontend: package.json with React/Vue/Angular, playwright.config.*
  • Backend: pyproject.toml, pom.xml, go.mod, *.csproj
  • Full-stack: Both indicators present

CI platform auto-detection - Checks for .github/workflows/, .gitlab-ci.yml, Jenkinsfile, and other CI config files.

Playwright Integration

Section titled “Playwright Integration”

TEA includes Playwright setup for browser testing:

Two MCP servers can be configured:

  1. playwright - npx @playwright/mcp@latest
  2. playwright-test - npx playwright run-test-mcp-server

After the Upgrade

Section titled “After the Upgrade”

The final output tells the full story:

28 AI tools now have BMAD configurations. That includes the original 18 plus newer additions: Kilo, Kiro, OpenCode, QwenCoder, Rovo Dev, Trae, Antigravity, iFlow, and others.

The last line is the one that matters: preserved settings. Your name, language, output directory, module preferences - all carried over.

The Full Tool Support List

Section titled “The Full Tool Support List”

BMAD v6.0.4 generates configurations for:

  • Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Windsurf (top tier)
  • Auggie, Cline, Crush, Gemini CLI
  • GitHub Copilot, Google Antigravity, iFlow
  • Kilo Code, Kiro, OpenCode, QwenCoder
  • Roo Cline, Rovo Dev, Trae

Each tool gets its own configuration directory with 27 agents, 74 workflows, and 7 tasks.

First Time? Start Here

Section titled “First Time? Start Here”

If you’re installing BMAD fresh:

Follow the interactive prompts. Pick at least the BMM and BMB modules. Add CIS if you do creative work, GDS for game dev, TEA for testing.

After installation, run /bmad-help in your AI coding tool to see what’s available.

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • Docs: https://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/
  • Changelog: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md
  • YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@BMadCode
  • Discord: https://discord.gg/gk8jAdXWmj

BMAD is 100% free. No paywalls, no gated Discord. If you find it useful, star the repo or subscribe to the YouTube channel.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Development
  • Vibe Coding
  • AI Agents
  • Testing

BMAD Phases + Ralph Execution Loop: A Unified AI Development Framework for Claude Code

Feb 14, 2026
Vibe Sparking

BMAD Phases + Ralph Execution Loop: A Unified AI Development Framework for Claude Code

Section titled “BMAD Phases + Ralph Execution Loop: A Unified AI Development Framework for Claude Code”

When coding with Claude Code, you’ve likely encountered two extremes:

“Vibe Coding” mode: Describe what you want, AI generates code, copy-paste. Fast, but as conversations grow long, the AI starts “forgetting” earlier decisions, and code quality becomes inconsistent.

“Over-ceremonial” mode: Install dozens of frameworks, configure endless templates, write extensive documentation. Before you write a single line of code, the afternoon is gone.

The combination of BMAD Method and Ralph Wiggum loop offers a middle path: structured development phases + autonomous execution loop. You get traceable decision records and hands-off execution capability.

Why a Unified Framework

Section titled “Why a Unified Framework”

Using BMAD or Ralph alone has limitations:

FrameworkStrengthsWeaknesses
BMADStructured process, role-based分工, traceable artifactsExecution phase requires human supervision
Ralph WiggumAutonomous execution, iterative refinement, hands-offLacks structure, prone to drifting from goals

Combine them, and BMAD provides the “what” and “why,” while Ralph provides the autonomous “how.”

The Four Phases of the Unified Framework

Section titled “The Four Phases of the Unified Framework”

Phase 1: Discovery

Section titled “Phase 1: Discovery”

Goal: Understand the problem space, gather context

BMAD’s Analyst agent owns this phase. Its responsibilities:

  • Clarify business requirements and technical constraints
  • Identify stakeholders and success criteria
  • Output: Brief document

Ralph Mode: Don’t use Ralph here. Discovery needs human involvement and judgment.

Phase 2: Planning

Section titled “Phase 2: Planning”

Goal: Create implementation plan, break down tasks

BMAD’s PM and Architect agents step in:

  • PM: Produces PRD (Product Requirements Document)
  • Architect: Produces technical architecture design
  • Output: PRD + Architecture Doc + Story List

Ralph Mode: Still no. Planning needs architectural decisions—too risky to automate.

Phase 3: Execution

Section titled “Phase 3: Execution”

Goal: Implement code according to Stories

This is where Ralph Wiggum shines.

Configuring Ralph Execution Loop:

Execution Mode:

  1. Ralph reads Story and acceptance criteria
  2. Generates code implementation
  3. Runs tests for verification
  4. If failed, auto-adjusts and retries
  5. Loops until all acceptance criteria pass

Key Configuration: Set clear exit conditions

Phase 4: Verification

Section titled “Phase 4: Verification”

Goal: End-to-end quality check

BMAD’s QA agent + Ralph work together:

  • QA: Defines verification scenarios and edge cases
  • Ralph: Auto-executes verification process

Case Study: Building an API Gateway

Section titled “Case Study: Building an API Gateway”

A team used this unified framework to refactor their API gateway:

Phase 1 (Discovery): 2 hours

  • Analyst agent mapped dependencies across 15 API endpoints
  • Output: Brief (12 pages)

Phase 2 (Planning): 4 hours

  • PM produced PRD (8 pages)
  • Architect produced architecture design (including comparison of 3 alternatives)
  • Story List: 12 Stories

Phase 3 (Execution): 3 days (unattended)

  • Ralph loop executed 12 Stories
  • Average 3-5 iterations per Story
  • Auto-generated test coverage: 87%

Phase 4 (Verification): 4 hours

  • QA defined 45 verification scenarios
  • Ralph auto-executed, found 3 edge case issues
  • Auto-fixed 2, manual fix for 1

Results:

  • Lines of code: 12,000+
  • Human intervention time: ~8 hours
  • Bug density: 0.3 per 1000 lines (vs industry average 15-50)

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”

Step 1: Install BMAD

Section titled “Step 1: Install BMAD”

When selecting modules, install at minimum:

  • Core
  • BMM (BMAD Method Module)

Step 2: Install Ralph Wiggum

Section titled “Step 2: Install Ralph Wiggum”

Step 3: Configure Execution Loop

Section titled “Step 3: Configure Execution Loop”

Create .bmad/ralph-config.yaml:

Step 4: Run Your First Workflow

Section titled “Step 4: Run Your First Workflow”

Caveats

Section titled “Caveats”

When NOT to Use Ralph

Section titled “When NOT to Use Ralph”

Not all tasks are suitable for autonomous loops:

  • Architectural decisions: Need human judgment
  • Security-related code: Needs manual review
  • UX changes: Need human verification
  • External API integrations: May incur real costs

Cost Control

Section titled “Cost Control”

Ralph loops consume API credits. Set guards:

Context Management

Section titled “Context Management”

Long loops cause context bloat. Solutions:

  1. Restart Ralph after each Story completes
  2. Use git as the memory layer
  3. Periodically run /compact to compress context

Comparison with Other Frameworks

Section titled “Comparison with Other Frameworks”
FrameworkStructure LevelAutonomous ExecutionLearning Curve
BMAD + RalphHighHighMedium
BMAD onlyHighLowMedium
Ralph onlyLowHighLow
GSDMediumMediumLow
Spec KitHighLowHigh

Summary

Section titled “Summary”

The BMAD phases + Ralph execution loop combination essentially splits software development into two layers:

  1. Decision Layer (BMAD): Human + AI collaboration, making judgments, setting direction
  2. Execution Layer (Ralph): AI autonomy, iterating per defined rules until completion

This layering gives you hands-off efficiency while maintaining control. Not fully hands-off, not micromanaging either.

Start with small tasks, validate the process, then scale to larger projects.

Resources:

  • BMAD Method GitHub
  • Ralph Wiggum Plugin
  • Spec-Driven Development Overview
Tags:
  • BMAD
  • Claude Code
  • Ralph Wiggum
  • AI Development
  • Spec-Driven Development
  • Automated Workflow

BMAD v6.0.0-beta-8 Installation Guide: Building Your AI Development Team from Scratch

Feb 14, 2026
Vibe Sparking

From Alpha to Beta: BMAD Has Grown Up

Section titled “From Alpha to Beta: BMAD Has Grown Up”

BMAD v6.0.0-beta-8 has been released. This isn’t just another update—BMAD has officially graduated from Alpha.

What does this mean?

  • 50+ workflows covering the complete development lifecycle
  • Stable architecture with no anticipated breaking changes
  • Support for custom modules and community extensions

More importantly: 100% free, no paywalls, no gated Discord.

Two Installation Methods: Which to Choose?

Section titled “Two Installation Methods: Which to Choose?”

Non-Interactive: For CI/CD Pipelines

Section titled “Non-Interactive: For CI/CD Pipelines”

If you’re installing in an automated environment, use command-line flags:

This command automatically installs:

  • BMM Module: Business Method Module for IT projects
  • BMB Module: BMad Builder for extending the platform

Then generates configuration files for 18 AI coding tools.

Interactive: For First-Time Users

Section titled “Interactive: For First-Time Users”

If you want to understand each option, run:

Then follow the prompts step by step.

Interactive Installation Walkthrough

Section titled “Interactive Installation Walkthrough”

Step 1: Choose Installation Directory

Section titled “Step 1: Choose Installation Directory”

The installer detects the current directory:

Confirm to continue.

Step 2: Select Modules

Section titled “Step 2: Select Modules”

BMAD offers 5 optional modules:

ModuleCodePurpose
BMad Method Agile-AI Driven-DevelopmentbmmTraditional software development
BMad BuilderbmbCreate custom agents and workflows
BMad Creative Intelligence SuitecisCreative writing, brainstorming
BMad Game Dev StudiogdsGame development
Test ArchitectteaTest strategy and quality assurance

My recommendation: Install at least bmm and bmb for your first time. Add cis if you write blogs or do creative work.

Step 3: Select AI Tool Integrations

Section titled “Step 3: Select AI Tool Integrations”

BMAD supports 18 AI coding tools:

  • ⭐ Claude Code (recommended)
  • ⭐ Codex
  • ⭐ Cursor
  • ⭐ Windsurf
  • Auggie
  • Cline
  • Crush
  • Gemini CLI
  • GitHub Copilot
  • Google Antigravity
  • iFlow
  • Kilo Code
  • Kiro
  • OpenCode
  • QwenCoder
  • Roo Cline
  • Rovo Dev
  • Trae

Choose the tools you actually use. Each tool gets its own configuration folder (like .claude/, .cursor/).

Step 4: Core Configuration

Section titled “Step 4: Core Configuration”

These settings affect how agents interact with you and where generated documents are saved.

Step 5: Module Configuration

Section titled “Step 5: Module Configuration”

Each module asks if you want to accept defaults:

Choose Yes for first-time installation. You can manually edit config files later if needed.

Installation Complete: What You Get

Section titled “Installation Complete: What You Get”

After installation, you’ll see a summary like this:

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

Q: Installation fails with IDE 'kiro-cli' handler not found

Section titled “Q: Installation fails with IDE 'kiro-cli' handler not found”

Remove the kiro-cli module from the installation command.

Q: Installation fails with Source for module 'bmgd' is not available

Section titled “Q: Installation fails with Source for module 'bmgd' is not available”

Remove bmgd and cis modules, then re-run the installation command.

After successful installation, you can add other modules:

After Installation: Next Steps

Section titled “After Installation: Next Steps”
  1. Run /bmad-help to see available commands and workflows
  2. Choose an agent to start your first task
  3. Read the docs at http://docs.bmad-method.org/

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • Documentation: http://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/
  • Changelog: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD/CHANGELOG.md
  • YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/@BMadCode

The BMAD team believes knowledge should be shared, not sold. If you find it useful, give them a Star or subscribe to their YouTube channel.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Development
  • Vibe Coding
  • AI Agents

BMAD Best Practices: AI-Driven Agile Development Guide

Feb 13, 2026
Vibe Sparking

BMAD Best Practices: AI-Driven Agile Development Guide

Section titled “BMAD Best Practices: AI-Driven Agile Development Guide”

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

Welcome to BMAD (Building Methodologies with AI Drivers) - an AI-driven Agile software development methodology that integrates AI capabilities into every phase of the development lifecycle. BMAD raises the baseline for development across different AI models, enabling teams to ship faster while maintaining quality.

What makes BMAD different?

  • AI-First Architecture: Built from the ground up for AI-assisted development
  • Flexible Workflows: From quick iterations to enterprise-grade processes
  • Living Documentation: Continuous updates, not static artifacts
  • Quality Automation: Automated testing, code review, and verification
  • Measurable Outcomes: Track progress with story points and sprint status

Quick Start: Installation and Setup

Section titled “Quick Start: Installation and Setup”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”
  • AI Coding Assistant: Claude Code, Cursor, or compatible AI tool
  • Model Requirements:
    • Recommended: Claude Opus 4.5 (200K context)
    • Alternative: Gemini 3 Pro (analysis) + Opus 4.5 (implementation)
    • Domestic: GLM 4.7 (200K) or MiniMax (192K)
  • Project Structure: Existing codebase or new project

Installation

Section titled “Installation”

BMAD installs per-project to isolate dependencies and configurations:

Directory structure created:

Core Workflows

Section titled “Core Workflows”

BMAD provides three workflow tiers based on project complexity and team needs:

1. Quick Development (/bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-dev)

Section titled “1. Quick Development (/bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-dev)”

Best for: Single features, bug fixes, rapid iterations

Workflow:

  1. Mode Detection: Tech-spec or direct instructions
  2. Context Gathering: Identify files, patterns, dependencies
  3. Execute: Implement tasks continuously
  4. Self-Check: Verify acceptance criteria
  5. Wrap-Up: Commit, document, notify

When to use:

  • ✅ Focused features (5-10 user stories, 15-50 story points)
  • ✅ Bug fixes with clear scope
  • ✅ Quick iterations with known patterns

Example:

2. Spec-Driven Development (/bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-spec)

Section titled “2. Spec-Driven Development (/bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-spec)”

Best for: Multi-component features requiring upfront planning

Workflow:

  1. Requirements Analysis: Clarify scope and constraints
  2. Tech Spec Creation: Document approach, tasks, acceptance criteria
  3. Review & Approval: Stakeholder sign-off
  4. Implementation: Execute via quick-dev
  5. Validation: Verify against spec

When to use:

  • ✅ Features spanning multiple components
  • ✅ Uncertain approach requiring exploration
  • ✅ Need stakeholder alignment before coding

3. Full BMM Process (Enterprise)

Section titled “3. Full BMM Process (Enterprise)”

Best for: Large epics, platform work, architectural changes

Complete lifecycle:

  • Initiation: /bmad:bmm:workflows:workflow-init
  • Analysis: Project briefing, requirements gathering
  • Planning: PRD → Architecture → UI/UX → Epics → Stories
  • Development: Story implementation with tests
  • Testing: Automated test coverage expansion
  • Code Review: Adversarial review process
  • Acceptance: Manual spot checks + automated verification
  • Retrospective: Post-epic learning capture

User Story Requirements: INVEST Criteria

Section titled “User Story Requirements: INVEST Criteria”

Every user story in BMAD must follow INVEST principles:

CriteriaMeaningExample
IndependentCan be developed in isolation”Add password reset” doesn’t depend on “Add 2FA”
NegotiableDetails can be discussed”Fast search” → negotiate definition of “fast”
ValuableDelivers user/business value”User can export data” > “Refactor export module”
EstimableCan estimate complexityClear enough to assign story points
SmallFits in one iterationCan complete in 1-3 days
TestableClear acceptance criteria”Search returns results in <2s with 10k records”

Story Points Scale:

  • 1-3: Simple feature, clear implementation
  • 5-8: Moderate complexity, some unknowns
  • 13+: Break into smaller stories

Development Workflow Patterns

Section titled “Development Workflow Patterns”

Daily Development: New Feature

Section titled “Daily Development: New Feature”

Example: Adding User Avatar Upload

  1. Assess complexity: Single component (profile page), known patterns
  2. Choose workflow: Quick-dev (direct mode)
  3. Execute:
  4. Automatic steps:
    • Context gathering (existing profile component, upload patterns)
    • Implementation (upload UI, validation, API integration)
    • Tests (unit tests for validation, integration test for upload)
    • Self-check (verify file size limits, format validation, preview display)
    • Commit & document

Daily Development: Bug Fix

Section titled “Daily Development: Bug Fix”

Example: Fix Login Timeout Issue

BMAD automatically:

  1. Searches codebase for session management
  2. Identifies timeout configuration
  3. Implements fix (extend timeout, add keep-alive)
  4. Adds regression test
  5. Verifies existing tests pass

Team Roles and Collaboration

Section titled “Team Roles and Collaboration”

Single-Role Model (OPE - One Person Entity)

Section titled “Single-Role Model (OPE - One Person Entity)”

Pros:

  • Maximum efficiency
  • No coordination overhead
  • Fast decision-making

Cons:

  • Single point of failure
  • Vulnerable to burnout
  • Limited perspective

Best for: Side projects, MVPs, proof-of-concepts

BMAD support:

  • Solo developer acts as PM + Dev + QA
  • AI handles requirements analysis, code generation, testing
  • Human focuses on decisions and creative direction

Multi-Role Model (3+ Team Members)

Section titled “Multi-Role Model (3+ Team Members)”

Roles:

  • Product Owner (PO) / Business Analyst (BA): Requirements, priorities, acceptance
  • Developer / Architect / DevOps: Implementation, architecture, operations
  • QA / Customer Service: Testing, user feedback, support

Pros:

  • Sustainable long-term
  • Diverse perspectives
  • Specialization benefits

Cons:

  • Coordination overhead
  • Communication challenges
  • Slower decision-making

Best for: Production systems, enterprise projects, long-term maintenance

BMAD support:

  • PO uses /bmad:bmm:workflows:prd for requirements
  • Architect uses /bmad:bmm:workflows:create-architecture
  • Dev uses /bmad:bmm:workflows:dev-story
  • QA uses /bmad:bmm:workflows:testarch-automate

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Keep UI/UX Consistent

Section titled “1. Keep UI/UX Consistent”

Approaches:

Option A: Upfront Design

  • Professional designers create drafts/prototypes
  • Implement UI code with mock data first
  • Integrate real data later
  • Focus on business value, not pixel perfection

Option B: Use Design Systems

  • Provide clear UI/UX guidelines and standards
  • Show examples of good/bad designs
  • Use skills like ui-ux-pro-max or frontend-design

Option C: Iterative Refinement

  • Build functional version quickly
  • Refine based on user feedback
  • ROI-driven improvements

2. Control Scope and Complexity

Section titled “2. Control Scope and Complexity”

Per iteration limits:

  • User Stories: 5-10 stories
  • Story Points: 15-50 points total
  • Duration: 1-2 weeks

Why this matters:

  • Fits within AI context windows
  • Reduces cognitive load
  • Enables quick pivots
  • Higher completion rates

Escalation signals:

  • Multiple components mentioned (dashboard + API + database)
  • System-level language (platform, integration, architecture)
  • Uncertainty about approach
  • Multi-layer scope (UI + backend + data)

Response:

  • BMAD detects complexity automatically
  • Suggests appropriate workflow
  • Offers planning before execution

3. Living Documents Over Static Specs

Section titled “3. Living Documents Over Static Specs”

Traditional approach:

BMAD approach:

Guidelines:

  • Update docs continuously during development
  • Mark obsolete sections explicitly
  • Treat completed stories as historical record (don’t modify)
  • Create new stories for changes/fixes

Example:

4. Adaptability to AI-Driven Development

Section titled “4. Adaptability to AI-Driven Development”

Key insight: BMAD cannot guarantee 100% automation of all requirements in one iteration.

Success factors:

  • Control scale: 5-10 stories per iteration
  • Manage complexity: 15-50 story points
  • Improve adaptability: Learn AI strengths/limitations
  • Iterate: Multiple cycles beat perfect plans

Reality check:

Project TypeExpected AutomationHuman Decisions Required
CRUD App80-90%Business rules, UX details
API Service70-80%Architecture choices, security
Data Pipeline60-70%Transformation logic, edge cases
Platform Work40-60%Design patterns, trade-offs

5. Quality Automation

Section titled “5. Quality Automation”

Testing pyramid:

Coverage goals:

  • Unit tests: 80%+ for business logic
  • Integration tests: Key workflows
  • E2E tests: Critical user paths
  • Manual checks: Edge cases, UX polish

Code review:

  • Adversarial review (finds issues actively)
  • Auto-fix option for minor issues
  • Focus on logic, security, maintainability

Frequently Asked Questions

Section titled “Frequently Asked Questions”

Installation & Setup

Section titled “Installation & Setup”

Q: Should BMAD be installed globally or per-project?

A: Per-project installation is recommended. This isolates dependencies, configurations, and workflows. Global installation may cause conflicts.

Q: Which AI model should I use?

A: Model capability and context length matter most. BMAD raises the baseline across models:

  • Global: Opus 4.5 (200K) or Gemini 3 Pro + Opus 4.5
  • Domestic: GLM 4.7 (200K) or MiniMax (192K)

Workflow Selection

Section titled “Workflow Selection”

Q: How do I choose between quick-dev and full BMM process?

A: Use this decision tree:

  1. Single file/component, clear approach → Quick-dev (direct)
  2. Multiple components, planning needed → Quick-spec
  3. Platform work, many unknowns → Full BMM
  4. “How should I…” or “Best way to…” → Start with planning

Q: Can I switch workflows mid-project?

A: Yes! BMAD workflows are composable:

  • Start with quick-dev, escalate to quick-spec if needed
  • Begin full BMM, use quick-dev for small follow-up tasks
  • Mix workflows based on task complexity

Requirements & Stories

Section titled “Requirements & Stories”

Q: Can I modify completed epics or stories?

A: Not recommended. Maintain them as historical records:

  • Mark obsolete sections explicitly
  • Create new stories for changes
  • Link to original story for context
  • Living documents ≠ mutable history

Q: What if requirements change after planning?

A: Use the course-correction workflow:

This helps navigate significant changes without losing momentum.

Team Structure

Section titled “Team Structure”

Q: Is BMAD suitable for solo developers?

A: Yes! Single-role model is explicitly supported:

  • AI acts as teammates (analyst, architect, tester)
  • Human focuses on decisions and creative direction
  • Faster than traditional solo development
  • Risk: vulnerability to burnout

Q: How does BMAD help teams collaborate?

A: BMAD reduces coordination overhead:

  • Clear role definitions and checkpoints
  • Shared artifacts in _bmad-output/
  • Automated handoffs between phases
  • Only human decisions require meetings

Scope & Limitations

Section titled “Scope & Limitations”

Q: Can BMAD implement all requirements automatically?

A: Not guaranteed, but BMAD significantly accelerates development:

  • Success depends on: scale, complexity, team adaptability
  • Control scope: 5-10 stories, 15-50 points per iteration
  • Iterative improvement beats waterfall planning
  • AI handles routine work; humans handle judgment calls

Q: Is BMAD suitable for OS, embedded, or firmware development?

A: BMAD is optimized for Agile-compatible projects:

  • Web/mobile apps: ✅ Excellent fit
  • APIs & services: ✅ Excellent fit
  • Data pipelines: ✅ Good fit
  • Desktop apps: ✅ Good fit
  • Embedded/firmware: ⚠️ Limited (if not Agile-compatible)
  • OS kernel work: ⚠️ Limited

Development Workflows

Section titled “Development Workflows”

Q: How do I develop new features in existing projects?

A: Three approaches:

  1. Quick features: /bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-dev "Add feature X"
  2. Planned features: /bmad:bmm:workflows:create-story then /bmad:bmm:workflows:dev-story
  3. YOLO mode: Direct execution without planning (experimental)

Q: How do I ensure UI/UX consistency?

A: Multiple strategies:

  • Use /bmad:bmm:workflows:create-ux-design for explicit design phase
  • Provide detailed design drafts/prototypes upfront
  • Establish clear UI/UX guidelines with examples
  • PO/BA/Designer implements UI directly with mock data
  • Focus on ROI, not pixel-perfect details

References

Section titled “References”
  • BMAD Official Documentation
  • Quick-Dev Workflow
  • BMM Project Levels
  • INVEST Criteria (Agile Alliance)
  • Story Points Estimation Guide

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”

Ready to try BMAD? Start with quick-dev:

Next steps:

  1. Install BMAD in your project
  2. Try quick-dev with a small feature
  3. Graduate to quick-spec for planned work
  4. Explore full BMM for complex projects
  5. Join the community and share learnings

BMAD continuously evolves. This guide reflects version 6.0.0-alpha.23. Check the official repository for updates.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI-Driven Development
  • Agile Methodology
  • Software Development
  • Best Practices
  • Development Workflow
  • Claude Code

BMad Installation Guide: Key Points and Common Pitfalls

Jan 25, 2026
Vibe Sparking

TL;DR: Run bunx bmad-method@alpha install in your project (NOT home directory), keep TTS disabled, choose project-specific Codex prompts, and install all recommended modules (BMB, BMGD, BMM, CIS). Here’s what you need to know before installing BMad v6.0.0-alpha.23.

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

BMad Method (Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development) just hit alpha.23, bringing 67 workflows, 25 agents, and a completely reorganized installation experience. If you’re installing BMad for the first time—or upgrading from an older version—here are the key points to watch out for.

1. Installation Directory: Don’t Install in Home

Section titled “1. Installation Directory: Don’t Install in Home”

First rule: don’t run the installer in your home directory.

The installer will confirm the resolved path and whether the directory is empty. This matters because BMad creates a _bmad folder with all its configuration, workflows, and agents.

2. AI Agents Selection: Recommended Tools Are Pre-Selected

Section titled “2. AI Agents Selection: Recommended Tools Are Pre-Selected”

During installation, you’ll be prompted to select AI tools to configure. The recommended tools are pre-selected for a reason—they’re the most stable and well-integrated options:

Recommended Tools (pre-selected):

  • Claude Code ⭐
  • Codex ⭐
  • Cursor ⭐
  • GitHub Copilot ⭐
  • Google Antigravity ⭐
  • OpenCode ⭐
  • Windsurf ⭐

Additional Tools (optional):

  • Atlassian Rovo Dev
  • Auggie CLI
  • Cline
  • Crush
  • Gemini CLI
  • iFlow CLI
  • Kilo Code
  • Kiro CLI
  • Qwen Code
  • Roo Code
  • Trae

If you’re just getting started, stick with the recommended tools. You can always add more later.

3. TTS: Keep It Disabled Unless You Need It

Section titled “3. TTS: Keep It Disabled Unless You Need It”

The installer asks if you want to enable Text-to-Speech:

Recommendation: Keep it disabled unless you’re specifically working alone on a local machine with audio output. TTS can surprise you with unexpected audio playback during collaborative sessions.

4. Module Selection: Install All Recommended Modules

Section titled “4. Module Selection: Install All Recommended Modules”

BMad includes four main modules. Install all of them unless you have a specific reason to skip one:

  • BMB (BMad Builder): Agent, Workflow, and Module Builder
  • BMGD (BMad Game Development): Game development workflows
  • BMM (BMad Method): Core Agile-AI-Driven-Development methodology
  • CIS (Creative Innovation Suite): Creativity workflows

Each module extends BMad’s capabilities. You can accept defaults for all of them during installation.

5. Codex CLI: Choose Project-Specific Installation

Section titled “5. Codex CLI: Choose Project-Specific Installation”

When configuring Codex, you’ll be asked where to install prompts:

Choose Project-specific. This ties prompts to your project and enables version control. You’ll need to set CODEX_HOME to use these prompts:

6. Core Configuration: Use Your Team Name

Section titled “6. Core Configuration: Use Your Team Name”

The installer asks for your name and language preferences:

If you’re working with a team, use a team name instead of your personal name—this makes AI responses more contextually appropriate for group collaboration.

7. What Gets Installed

Section titled “7. What Gets Installed”

After a successful installation, you’ll have:

The installer generates:

  • 67 workflows for various development scenarios
  • 25 agents with specialized roles
  • 96 configuration files across all tools
  • Tool-specific configs for Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Copilot, and more

8. Upgrading from Older Versions

Section titled “8. Upgrading from Older Versions”

If you’re upgrading from a version older than alpha.21, you’ll need a fresh install:

  1. Remove the _bmad folder
  2. Reinstall using bunx bmad-method@alpha install

This is only necessary during the final push to beta (ETA January 18th, but already past as of this writing). After beta, upgrades should be smoother.

9. Verification and Next Steps

Section titled “9. Verification and Next Steps”

After installation:

  1. Check the _bmad folder exists in your project root
  2. Review the generated workflows in _bmad/bmm/workflows/
  3. Set up your IDE (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) to use the BMad patterns
  4. Read the docs at docs.bmad-method.org

Your first workflow should be quick-dev for rapid feature implementation or quick-spec for planning before coding.

Common Pitfalls to Avoid

Section titled “Common Pitfalls to Avoid”
PitfallSolution
Installing in home directoryAlways cd to project first
Skipping recommended toolsThey’re pre-selected for stability
Enabling TTS accidentallyKeep disabled unless solo work
Choosing global Codex promptsProject-specific enables version control
Using personal name for team projectsUse a team name instead

Closing

Section titled “Closing”

BMad v6.0.0-alpha.23 is a significant upgrade with better architecture, more workflows, and improved tool integration. The installation is straightforward once you know these key points.

If you run into issues, check the changelog or open an issue on GitHub.

What’s your experience with BMad installation? Any other tips to share?

Tags:
  • BMad
  • AI Development
  • Agile
  • Installation Guide

BMad Quick Dev Workflow: Building a Game in 2.5 Hours

Jan 25, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

Last week, I decided to test the BMad Method’s Quick Dev workflow by building something from scratch: a stickman fighting game using Phaser 3. The goal was simple—see how much AI could autonomously handle, and where human intervention was actually needed.

Two and a half hours later, I had a complete playable game with:

  • Procedurally rendered stickman characters (no sprite assets needed)
  • Player controls (A/D move, W jump, J punch, K kick)
  • An AI opponent with approach/attack/retreat behavior
  • Health bars, hit detection, and knockback physics

Here’s how it went, step by step.

What is Quick Dev?

Section titled “What is Quick Dev?”

Quick Dev is BMad’s streamlined workflow for focused implementation tasks. It’s designed for situations where you either:

  • Have a tech-spec ready to execute, or
  • Know exactly what you want to build and want to dive straight in

The workflow uses a “step-file architecture”—each phase loads fresh to combat the “lost in the middle” problem that plagues long AI sessions. State persists through variables rather than context, allowing for marathon coding sessions without degradation.

The 6-Step Process

Section titled “The 6-Step Process”

Step 1: Mode Detection

Section titled “Step 1: Mode Detection”

What happens: The workflow captures a baseline commit (for diff tracking), loads project context if available, and determines whether you’re working from a tech-spec or direct instructions.

Human intervention required:

When I ran /bmad:bmm:workflows:quick-dev with the prompt “build a stickman fighting game in Phaser 3,” the workflow analyzed the scope and presented three options:

My choice: I selected [e] - Execute directly. I wanted to see how far AI could go without formal planning.

Why this matters: This checkpoint prevents AI from committing to approaches that might be wrong. For larger projects (platforms, multi-system work), the workflow would have escalated me to full BMad Method planning.

Step 2: Context Gathering

Section titled “Step 2: Context Gathering”

What happens: The workflow analyzes the request to identify:

  • Files to modify (or create, for new projects)
  • Relevant patterns (from existing code or frameworks)
  • Dependencies (external libraries, internal modules)
  • A mental plan with tasks and acceptance criteria

What was presented:

Human intervention required: Confirmation to proceed. I could have adjusted the plan, but it looked solid.

Step 3: Execute

Section titled “Step 3: Execute”

What happens: This is where the magic happens. The workflow implements tasks continuously—stopping only for blocking issues, not for approval between tasks.

Key principle: Do NOT halt for milestones. Execute all tasks in sequence.

What I watched happen:

The AI created a complete project structure:

Then it built each component:

  1. Project scaffolding - Vite + TypeScript + Phaser 3.80.1
  2. Stickman class - Procedural rendering using Phaser Graphics API. Each body part (head, torso, arms, legs) drawn as lines with joints
  3. Animation system - Pose-based animation with smooth transitions between idle, walk, jump, punch, kick states
  4. Combat system - Hitbox detection, damage calculation, knockback physics
  5. AI opponent - Simple state machine: approach when far, attack when close, retreat when health low

Human intervention required: None during execution. The workflow found a minor issue with jump animation (leg movement too subtle) and fixed it autonomously.

Total tokens used: ~155,000 Time: About 90 minutes of continuous work

Step 4: Self-Check

Section titled “Step 4: Self-Check”

What happens: Before presenting results, the workflow self-audits:

  • Are all tasks complete?
  • Do tests pass?
  • Are acceptance criteria satisfied?
  • Were existing patterns followed?

What was presented:

Human intervention required: None—this is automatic. But if anything had failed, the workflow would have halted.

Step 5: Adversarial Review

Section titled “Step 5: Adversarial Review”

What happens: The workflow constructs a diff of all changes and invokes an adversarial code reviewer—a separate agent that has only the diff, no context. This information asymmetry forces the reviewer to find real problems, not excuse things based on “what the AI was thinking.”

The process:

  1. Git diff from baseline commit
  2. New untracked files included
  3. Separate subagent reviews only the diff
  4. Findings categorized by severity and validity

What was found:

The adversarial reviewer found 3 findings:

IDSeverityValidityDescription
F1LowRealJump pose hip offset hardcoded to 2, should use direction
F2LowRealBody lean during jump too subtle (0.05), hard to notice
F3TrivialNoiseComment style inconsistency

Human intervention required: None yet—findings are presented for resolution in next step.

Step 6: Resolve Findings

Section titled “Step 6: Resolve Findings”

What happens: You choose how to handle the findings:

Human intervention required: Decision on resolution approach.

My choice: [2] - Auto-fix. The findings were minor and the AI understood what needed fixing.

What was fixed:

  • F1: Changed hipOffsetX: dir * 2 to include both direction and air phase
  • F2: Increased body lean from 0.05 to 0.1 + air phase adjustment
  • F3: Skipped (noise)

Final result:

The Game That Was Built

Section titled “The Game That Was Built”

Here’s what 2.5 hours produced:

The complete game is about 600 lines of TypeScript, with:

  • Smooth pose-based animation
  • Responsive keyboard controls
  • Basic AI that actually fights back
  • Health UI and game-over state

What AI Did Autonomously

Section titled “What AI Did Autonomously”
PhaseTasksHuman Input
SetupProject initialization, dependency installation1 choice (execute mode)
AnalysisScope breakdown, task planning1 confirmation
Implementation600+ lines of code, all files0 interventions
TestingBuild verification, runtime testing0 interventions
ReviewAdversarial analysis, 3 findings found0 interventions
FixesApplied 2 fixes automatically1 choice (auto-fix)

Where Humans Are Still Needed

Section titled “Where Humans Are Still Needed”

After this experiment, here’s where human input proved essential:

  1. Initial direction - The workflow can’t decide what to build
  2. Mode selection - Understanding whether formal planning is needed
  3. Complex decisions - Major architectural choices still benefit from human judgment
  4. Creative direction - Art style, game feel, these are human domains

Key Takeaways

Section titled “Key Takeaways”
  1. Step-file architecture works - 2.5 hours of continuous execution without context degradation
  2. Adversarial review finds real issues - The separate reviewer caught subtle bugs the main agent missed
  3. YOLO mode is viable for focused work - With proper guardrails, AI can execute autonomously
  4. Human attention is precious - The workflow preserves it for decision points, not implementation

Would I Use This Again?

Section titled “Would I Use This Again?”

Absolutely. For focused implementation tasks—features, refactorings, bug fixes—Quick Dev is significantly faster than traditional coding. The adversarial review catches issues I would have missed, and the step-file architecture enables marathon sessions.

Would I use it for a platform rewrite? No—that’s what the full BMad Method is for. But for a game, a feature, a service? Quick Dev delivers.

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • BMad Method Documentation: https://docs.bmad-method.org/
  • BMad GitHub: https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
  • Phaser 3: https://phaser.io/
  • Terminal log of this session: .terminal-logs/terminal_20260124_210313_1769259793512.log

This game was built using BMad Method v6.0.0-alpha.23 with Claude Opus 4.5. Total time from blank directory to playable game: approximately 2.5 hours.

Tags:
  • BMad
  • AI-Assisted-Development
  • Phaser
  • Game-Development
  • Workflow
  • Quick-Dev

BMAD Architecture Deep Dive: 26 Agents, 68 Workflows, and 655 Files

Jan 15, 2026
Vibe Sparking

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

BMAD (Business Method Agent Development) represents a paradigm shift in how we approach software development and project management. It’s not just another AI assistant—it’s a comprehensive multi-agent system with 26 specialized agents, 68 registered workflows, 4 core tasks, and 655 files working in harmony.

This article takes you deep into BMAD’s architecture, revealing how its modular design enables specialized expertise across business/IT projects, game development, creative innovation, and system building.

System Overview: Six Modules

Section titled “System Overview: Six Modules”

BMAD’s architecture is organized into six main modules, each serving a distinct purpose:

1. Agents: 26 Specialized Personas

Section titled “1. Agents: 26 Specialized Personas”

Core Module (1 Agent)

Section titled “Core Module (1 Agent)”

bmad-master - The master executor and workflow orchestrator that coordinates all other agents.

BMM Module: Business & IT (9 Agents)

Section titled “BMM Module: Business & IT (9 Agents)”

The Business Method Module contains agents for traditional software development:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
analystMaryBusiness analyst, market research, requirements
architectWinstonSystem architect, technical design
devAmeliaSenior software engineer, implementation
pmJohnProduct manager, PRD creation
smBobScrum master, agile ceremonies
teaMuratMaster test architect, quality assurance
tech-writerPaigeTechnical documentation specialist
ux-designerSallyUX/UI designer
quick-flow-solo-devBarryFull-stack developer with Quick Flow focus

BMB Module: System Builders (3 Agents)

Section titled “BMB Module: System Builders (3 Agents)”

For extending the BMAD platform itself:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
agent-builderBondAgent architecture specialist
module-builderMorganModule architecture specialist
workflow-builderWendyWorkflow architecture specialist

BMGD Module: Game Development (6 Agents)

Section titled “BMGD Module: Game Development (6 Agents)”

Specialized agents for game creation:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
game-architectCloud DragonbornGame systems architect
game-designerSamus ShepardLead game designer
game-devLink FreemanGame developer
game-qaGLaDOSGame QA architect
game-scrum-masterMaxGame development scrum master
game-solo-devIndieElite indie game developer

CIS Module: Creative Innovation (6 Agents)

Section titled “CIS Module: Creative Innovation (6 Agents)”

For creativity and innovation:

AgentPersonaSpecialization
brainstorming-coachCarsonMaster brainstorming facilitator
creative-problem-solverDr. QuinnSystematic problem-solving expert
design-thinking-coachMayaHuman-centered design expert
innovation-strategistVictorBusiness model innovator
presentation-masterCaravaggioVisual communication expert
storytellerSophiaMaster storyteller

Agent Characteristics

Section titled “Agent Characteristics”

Each BMAD agent features:

  • Unique persona with distinct communication style
  • Domain expertise in specific areas
  • BMAD compliance standards
  • Menu-based interface for workflow execution
  • Multi-language support with configuration-driven communication

2. Workflows: 68 Registered + 481 Implementation Files

Section titled “2. Workflows: 68 Registered + 481 Implementation Files”

Core Workflows (2)

Section titled “Core Workflows (2)”
  • brainstorming - Interactive brainstorming sessions
  • party-mode - Multi-agent conversation orchestration

BMM Workflows: Business Method (22)

Section titled “BMM Workflows: Business Method (22)”

Covering the complete software development lifecycle:

Analysis Phase:

  • research - Comprehensive research across domains
  • create-product-brief - Product brief discovery

Planning Phase:

  • create-ux-design - UX patterns and design planning
  • prd - PRD creation, validation, and editing

Solutioning Phase:

  • create-architecture - Architecture decision facilitation
  • create-epics-and-stories - Transform PRDs into actionable stories
  • check-implementation-readiness - Pre-implementation validation

Implementation Phase:

  • code-review - Adversarial senior developer review
  • dev-story - Execute user stories with implementation
  • sprint-planning - Sprint status tracking
  • retrospective - Post-epic review and lessons learned

Quick Flow:

  • quick-dev - Flexible development execution
  • quick-spec - Conversational spec engineering

BMB Workflows: Builders (3)

Section titled “BMB Workflows: Builders (3)”
  • agent - Tri-modal agent creation/editing
  • module - Quad-modal module creation
  • workflow - Workflow creation architecture

BMGD Workflows: Game Development (29)

Section titled “BMGD Workflows: Game Development (29)”

The most comprehensive suite for game development:

Preproduction:

  • brainstorm-game - Game-specific brainstorming
  • create-game-brief - Game brief through discovery
  • game-brief - Game brief creation

Design:

  • create-gdd - Game Design Document creation
  • gdd - Game Design Document workflows
  • narrative - Narrative design workflows

Technical:

  • game-architecture - Game architecture workflow
  • generate-project-context - Project context generation

Production:

  • code-review - Game-specific code reviews
  • correct-course - Sprint course correction
  • dev-story - Game story execution
  • retrospective - Game development retrospective
  • sprint-planning - Game sprint planning
  • sprint-status - Game sprint status

Quick Flow:

  • quick-dev - Flexible game development
  • quick-prototype - Rapid prototyping
  • quick-spec - Game spec engineering

Testing (6 workflows):

  • gametest-automate - Automated game test generation
  • gametest-performance - Performance testing
  • gametest-playtest-plan - Playtest planning
  • gametest-test-design - Test design
  • gametest-framework - Test framework initialization
  • gametest-test-review - Test quality review

Workflow Management:

  • workflow-init - Initialize BMAD game projects
  • workflow-status - Status checking

CIS Workflows: Creative Innovation (12)

Section titled “CIS Workflows: Creative Innovation (12)”

Innovation and creativity workflows:

  • Brainstorming sessions
  • Design thinking facilitation
  • Problem-solving workshops
  • Innovation strategy sessions

Workflow Architecture Patterns

Section titled “Workflow Architecture Patterns”

BMAD employs several architectural patterns:

  1. Step-file architecture - Each step loads fresh to combat “lost in the middle” syndrome
  2. Micro-file architecture - Sequential conversation orchestration
  3. Tri-modal workflows - Create, Validate, Edit capabilities
  4. Quad-modal workflows - Brief, Create, Edit, Validate

3. Tasks: 4 Core Tasks

Section titled “3. Tasks: 4 Core Tasks”

Tasks are implemented as XML files in the core module:

TaskDescription
workflowExecute workflow configurations
review-adversarial-generalCynical content review
shard-docSplit large documents into smaller files
index-docsGenerate/update documentation indexes

4. Files: 655 Total Files

Section titled “4. Files: 655 Total Files”

File Distribution

Section titled “File Distribution”
TypeCountPurpose
Markdown (.md)537Documentation, templates, workflows
YAML (.yaml)80Configuration files, workflow definitions
CSV (.csv)24Manifests and data files
XML (.xml)11Task definitions
JSON (.json)2Configuration data

Key Manifest Files

Section titled “Key Manifest Files”
  • agent-manifest.csv - 27 registered agents with metadata
  • workflow-manifest.csv - 68 registered workflows
  • task-manifest.csv - 4 core tasks
  • files-manifest.csv - Complete file inventory (655 files)
  • tool-manifest.csv - Available tools and capabilities

Notable Architectural Patterns

Section titled “Notable Architectural Patterns”

1. Modular Design

Section titled “1. Modular Design”

Clear separation of concerns across domains:

  • Each module operates independently
  • Shared core functionality
  • Extensible architecture

2. Manifest-Driven System

Section titled “2. Manifest-Driven System”

CSV manifests provide:

  • Complete inventory of components
  • Metadata for each agent/workflow/task
  • Version tracking and management

3. Workflow-Centric Execution

Section titled “3. Workflow-Centric Execution”

Agents execute through structured workflows:

  • Consistent patterns across modules
  • State management via variables
  • Sequential progression through phases

4. Quality Assurance Built-In

Section titled “4. Quality Assurance Built-In”
  • Adversarial code reviews in all modules
  • Implementation readiness validation
  • Test architecture specialists
  • Sprint retrospectives and corrections

Recommended Use Cases

Section titled “Recommended Use Cases”

BMM Best For

Section titled “BMM Best For”
  • Traditional software development projects
  • Business analysis and requirement gathering
  • Enterprise application development
  • Teams following agile methodologies

BMGD Best For

Section titled “BMGD Best For”
  • Game development (Unity, Unreal, Godot)
  • Indie game developers
  • Game design documentation
  • Game testing and quality assurance

BMB Best For

Section titled “BMB Best For”
  • Creating custom BMAD agents
  • Building new BMAD modules
  • Workflow architecture design
  • System integration

CIS Best For

Section titled “CIS Best For”
  • Innovation workshops
  • Design thinking sessions
  • Problem-solving breakthroughs
  • Creative brainstorming

Unique Features

Section titled “Unique Features”

Party Mode

Section titled “Party Mode”

Multi-agent conversations for complex decision-making through agent collaboration.

Quick Flow

Section titled “Quick Flow”

Rapid development methodologies for fast iteration and prototyping.

Game-Specific Tools

Section titled “Game-Specific Tools”

Specialized workflows for game development needs, from design to testing.

Creative Suite

Section titled “Creative Suite”

Innovation and design thinking tools for creative problem-solving.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

BMAD represents a sophisticated approach to multi-agent systems, with:

  • 26 agents across 4 specialized domains
  • 68 workflows covering complete development lifecycles
  • 4 core tasks for system operations
  • 655 files implementing the entire platform

The modular design, manifest-driven architecture, and workflow-centric execution make BMAD a powerful tool for teams looking to leverage AI agents across diverse domains—from traditional software development to game creation and creative innovation.

Whether you’re building enterprise applications, developing games, or facilitating innovation workshops, BMAD has specialized agents and workflows designed to help you succeed.

References

Section titled “References”
  • BMAD Documentation
  • BMAD Getting Started Guide
  • BMAD Upgrade Experience
Tags:
  • BMAD
  • AI Agents
  • Workflow Automation
  • Game Development
