  • BMAD
  • Claude Code
  • AI Agent
  • AI Development
  • Agile Development

BMAD v6 Installation Update: Modularization + AgentVibes “talking” dev experience

Dec 15, 2025
Vibe Sparking

The core changes in v6 are a rebuilt directory/artifact system plus voice (AgentVibes) formally integrated into the BMAD workflow.

1) Bottom line first: the two most tangible changes from v4 to v6

Section titled “1) Bottom line first: the two most tangible changes from v4 to v6”

Change A: the directory moved from “single core” to an engineering structure of “Core + Modules + Manifests.” In the v4 user guide, personalization preferences (tech stack, naming rules, etc.) were managed at .bmad-core/data/technical-preferences.md.

In v6 (Alpha), the official positioning is “Agent-as-Code + reproducible pipelines,” with an explicit workspace anatomy: .bmad/ stores profiles/blueprints/runs and other traceable artifacts.

Change B: AgentVibes is treated as the first-party experience layer: letting BMAD agents speak in Claude Code, switch voices, and be controlled by slash commands / MCP. AgentVibes’ official description is blunt: it was originally built to “add voice” to Claude Code and offers both MCP (natural-language control) and slash commands.

2) Directory structure: v4 “.bmad-core” vs v6 “.bmad + modules + artifacts”

Section titled “2) Directory structure: v4 “.bmad-core” vs v6 “.bmad + modules + artifacts””

Breaking it down based on the installation log

v4 (common mental model for users)

Section titled “v4 (common mental model for users)”
  • Core directory: .bmad-core/ Stores preferences, data, templates; for example, the v4 user guide clearly points to .bmad-core/data/technical-preferences.md for preferences.
  • Web bundle workflow: dist/teams/... The typical move is to copy dist/teams/team-fullstack.txt into a web UI/Claude/GPT as a “team prompt.”
  • Feels like a “prompt framework + directory convention.” Engineering-wise it works, but “traceable/reproducible outputs” were not the primary goal.

v6 (what your installation shows)

Section titled “v6 (what your installation shows)”

From the log, the most important “on-disk” changes are:

  • Root directory unified at <project>/.bmad (yours: /Users/vsai/.../vs-ai-agents/.bmad)
  • Modular installation: Core + bmb + bmgd + bmm + cis
    • Core: installs core agents (e.g., bmad-master.md)
    • bmb: Builder/Factory (the “factory” for custom agents, workflows, modules)
    • bmm: Method (Analyst/PM/Dev/SM/UX, the full team)
    • bmgd: Game Dev (and vendors cross-module workflows)
    • cis: Creative Innovation Suite

At the same time, it makes a few things explicit that weren’t very visible in v4:

  • Compiles/generates artifacts (manifests): the log literally reports “61 workflows, 21 agents, 5 tasks, 1 tools…” — evidence that v6 is leaning into “versionable/traceable” outputs (much more engineered). That matches the v6 Alpha emphasis on “reproducible, auditable, diffable.”
  • Sprint artifact directory becomes an installation-time setting: you chose docs/sprint-artifacts, and the installer created it (handy for teams running agile).
  • Custom extension directories are explicitly separated
    • bmad-custom-src: custom agents/workflows
    • bmad-custom-modules-src: custom modules
      This essentially forces separation of “official modules” and “custom assets” for easier upgrades/merges.
  • Sidecar memory directory: the log also replaced {agent_sidecar_folder} → {project-root}/.bmad-user-memory (typically for agent “sidecar memory/run traces,” aligning with v6’s artifact-centric approach).

v4 is more “prompt framework + directory convention”; v6 is more “agent engineering system with build artifacts and traceable assets.”

3) AgentVibes integration: the key upgrades from “usable” to “pleasant”

Section titled “3) AgentVibes integration: the key upgrades from “usable” to “pleasant””

The most attention-grabbing part of the log is this:

  • Installer showed “AgentVibes TTS Injection Summary”
  • Injected TTS into .../.bmad/core/agents/bmad-master.md
  • Backups stored at ~/.bmad-tts-backups/
  • Then launched the AgentVibes installer, dropping slash commands, TTS hooks, personality templates, background music, etc., into the project’s .claude/

This matches AgentVibes’ official description closely:

What problem does AgentVibes solve?

Section titled “What problem does AgentVibes solve?”
  • It provides MCP (natural-language control): assistants don’t have to memorize slash commands; you can just say “switch to the Aria voice.”
  • It also provides slash commands (zero token cost) like /agent-vibes:list, /agent-vibes:switch Aria.
  • Multi–TTS-provider abstraction: Piper, macOS Say, ElevenLabs are all supported in the same capability layer (your log shows the macOS Say fast-install path).
  • The installer explicitly states it will put commands/hooks/personalities/plugins into .claude/.

Why does this matter for BMAD v6?

Section titled “Why does this matter for BMAD v6?”

Because v6 targets “multi-agent, multi-workflow, multi-artifact,” humans’ attention gets fragmented across lots of process info. TTS brings two benefits:

  1. Stronger state feedback: start/finish cues are audible — no need to stare at the screen.
  2. Stronger sense of roles: switching PM/SM/Architect/Dev roles feels more like a team (AgentVibes’ BMAD integration highlights this).

4) Summary for moving from v4 to v6

Section titled “4) Summary for moving from v4 to v6”

  1. Plan how to migrate v4 preferences v4 preferences live at .bmad-core/data/technical-preferences.md. v6 emphasizes workspace + policies + manifests (the structure under .bmad/). Recommendation: keep the v4 preference content as “team conventions,” then gradually map it to v6’s profile/policy system (avoid a one-shot forced migration that loses meaning).

  2. Put “custom assets” into the two directories you’ve configured

  • agents/workflows → bmad-custom-src
  • modules → bmad-custom-modules-src
    This reduces conflicts when upgrading v6 alpha/beta later.
  1. Default to “slash commands first” for AgentVibes AgentVibes also notes: MCP is convenient but consumes context tokens; slash commands are cheaper. In other words: use /-commands for daily work, then fall back to natural-language MCP occasionally for a balanced experience and cost.

Detailed log

Section titled “Detailed log”
Tags:
  • BMAD

Will Documentation-Driven AI Coding Become the New 'Spaghetti Mountain'? Pros and Cons of BMAD, Spec-Kit, and OpenSpec

Oct 23, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Remember the days when “spaghetti code💩” used to give everyone a headache? With AI copilots like Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini in the mix, we have stumbled into a brand-new pitfall—the “documentation spaghetti💩.” Documentation-driven AI programming has become a hot trend: write a clear specification first, then let AI generate the code. It sounds wonderful—no more guessing what the model wants, just provide a well-written brief and code appears automatically. The truth, though, is that if you take the wrong approach, the documentation can pile up into a brand-new spaghetti mountain that leaves you laughing and crying at the same time 😅.

Introduction: What on Earth Are BMAD, Spec-Kit, and OpenSpec?

Section titled “Introduction: What on Earth Are BMAD, Spec-Kit, and OpenSpec?”

Let’s start with these documentation-driven AI programming tools that are making the rounds—they each have their own personality:

BMAD Method – a “big and comprehensive” framework whose full name is Breakthrough Method for Agile AI-Driven Development. Its ambitions are huge: it tries to simulate a complete agile squad with AI. There is an analyst AI, an architect AI, a developer AI, a tester AI—each playing a role so that planning, coding, and testing all get staged like a full production. BMAD ships with a CLI and a web UI, plus special “script files” and context management so the AI team can collaborate. It usually unfolds in two phases: first the “analyst/product manager/architect” agents do the planning and design, then the “Scrum Master/developer” agents write the actual code. The upside is a thorough workflow that stays orderly—ideal for large, complex projects that demand clear documentation and division of labor. The downside is that it is cumbersome to use, has a steep learning curve, and expects the underlying model to truly understand the documentation you write.

Spec-Kit – an open-source utility on GitHub that layers a bit of “process” on top of your existing AI coding assistant. Developers use the command line to nudge AI through a “Specification → Plan → Tasks → Implementation” four-step. In short, Spec-Kit asks you to nail the requirements first, then guides AI to break work down and produce code. It turns your specification markdown into an “executable document” using commands like /specify, /plan, and /tasks. It is lightweight and flexible, does not force you to revamp your workflow, and is model-agnostic. Great for solo builders or small teams that want to add structure and improve AI-generated code quality.

OpenSpec – a minimalist newcomer that believes “simple is better.” You write a slim Markdown spec and pair it with a handful of commands to finish the job. Its flow has only three steps: submit a change proposal, let AI produce the task list, then let AI apply the spec to modify the code. The highlight is efficiency: the specs stay lean, token usage stays low, and there is barely any metadata. It works well for individuals or small teams chasing extreme speed—minor tweaks get done in minutes. The trade-off is that model support is still limited, but it is so easy to learn that there is almost no overhead.

Spot the pattern? These tools share the same philosophy: document first, then drive the AI. The goal is to reduce the uncertainty of vibe coding by giving AI a clear rulebook so code quality becomes controllable. Ideal in theory—but reality bites. If you are not careful, the documentation you create can morph into another giant spaghetti pile. Why does that happen? Read on.

Four Reasons Documentation Turns into a Spaghetti Mountain Even Faster

Section titled “Four Reasons Documentation Turns into a Spaghetti Mountain Even Faster”

1. Lack of Structure

Section titled “1. Lack of Structure”

Code is constrained by syntax—one misplaced comma and it will not even run. Documentation is much freer, and AI is not human. When a model stares at free-form, meandering text, its comprehension goes sideways. Code is deterministic; specifications are often vague and open to interpretation. If your documentation is loosely structured, the AI sees the world through fogged glass, and it is no surprise when the code it generates veers off in the wrong direction.

2. Inconsistent AI Understanding

Section titled “2. Inconsistent AI Understanding”

Even if you write a decent document, there is no guarantee the AI will interpret it the same way every time. Seasoned developers know the saying “a thousand readers, a thousand Hamlets”—and AI thinks even more differently from humans. ChatGPT might implement the feature correctly today, but a different model tomorrow could distort your intent. Documentation lacks the compiler’s safety net, so misunderstandings can hide in plain sight and quietly plant landmines.

3. AI Hallucinations Amplify Errors

Section titled “3. AI Hallucinations Amplify Errors”

We all know large language models hallucinate—confidently fabricating details. When AI uses incomplete or ambiguous documentation to write code, the stakes rise. Miss a detail in your spec and the AI might “fill in the gaps” with something that looks plausible but is wrong. Worse still, if that flawed AI-generated documentation or code gets reused later, another AI might treat it as gospel truth. Errors balloon like rumors: once they make it into formal docs, people and models alike will keep getting misled.

4. New Risks Introduced by the Tools Themselves

Section titled “4. New Risks Introduced by the Tools Themselves”

Finally, consider the complexity of the tools. Adopting Spec-Kit or BMAD might require extra software and brand-new rituals. You can end up wrestling with tooling, pouring hours into crafting specs and calibrating workflows, while actual coding time shrinks. If you do not internalize the framework, you may copy templates mechanically, letting documentation drift away from the code. Like wielding a scalpel without training—the damage can be greater than doing nothing.

How to Avoid the Pile-Up: A Tiered Playbook for Documentation Tools

Section titled “How to Avoid the Pile-Up: A Tiered Playbook for Documentation Tools”

The pitfalls sound daunting, but do not lose heart. With a tiered usage strategy, documentation-driven programming can still pave a smoother road:

Use direct prompts for tiny tweaks

Section titled “Use direct prompts for tiny tweaks”

When you are only adjusting a small logic branch or styling tweak, there is no need to assemble a grand spec. In these cases, asking Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, GitHub Copilot, or Cursor directly is fastest. Explain the request in a sentence or two and let the AI offer a diff or snippet. You save time and stay focused. As veterans like to say: small asks do not need the full ceremony; if a quick chat solves it, skip the paperwork.

Reach for Spec-Kit or OpenSpec on medium-sized changes

Section titled “Reach for Spec-Kit or OpenSpec on medium-sized changes”

If you are adding a mid-sized feature—multiple steps, not massive—consider a lightweight tool such as Spec-Kit or OpenSpec. Spend ten minutes drafting an MVP-level spec that captures key requirements and edge cases, then hand it to the AI to refine the plan and tasks. Spec-Kit decomposes work item by item; OpenSpec minimizes context switching and handles the change in a few commands. The key is to keep the spec concise, covering only what is necessary. Data shows that compact specs are easier for AI to execute faithfully and more efficient overall. Once the feature ships, remember to update canonical docs or clean up temporary specs to prevent outdated information from piling up.

Deploy BMAD for major overhauls

Section titled “Deploy BMAD for major overhauls”

For large-scale refactors or brand-new complex subsystems—the real “surgery”—you need a rigorous approach. This is where a multi-agent framework like BMAD earns its keep: it orchestrates end-to-end planning, architecture, and task allocation. Think of it as hiring a virtual AI team to keep big projects on the rails. Of course, humans must stay in the loop—never blindly accept each AI output; review and adjust. Before spinning up BMAD, weigh the costs: it takes time and demands strong model comprehension. That investment can pay off for long-lived initiatives, but the documentation it produces—specs, user stories, and more—should evolve with the code. Do not toss it aside after one run; maintain it alongside the project or you will be rebuilding from scratch next time.

No matter the scale, remember the “documentation MVP” principle: the doc should cover only what is necessary and true right now. Do not chase completeness for its own sake. Many veterans delete temporary specs once a feature is done because the code itself is already clear. Instead of hoarding outdated notes that mislead people, keep a lean, accurate reference and update it as the product evolves. Documentation earns its value by reflecting reality; once it drifts, it is just dead weight.

Conclusion

Section titled “Conclusion”

Documentation-driven programming is a double-edged sword in the AI era. Used well, it makes development more efficient and orderly; misused, it spawns fresh spaghetti mountains. Ambitious young engineers should absolutely explore new tools, but not worship them blindly. Whether we are talking code or docs, the heart of the matter is understanding the requirement and keeping information current. Whenever you decide to write a spec or pick a tool, ask yourself: “Is this making the problem clearer—or just adding complexity?”

Here’s to every developer mastering these AI tools, producing docs that stay crisp and useful, and turning documentation into an asset—not a burden. We all want higher productivity, not a literal spaghetti mountain blocking the office! 💩

Tags:
  • AI
  • BMAD
  • Spec-Kit
  • OpenSpec
  • Documentation-Oriented Programming

Use BMAD in Codex like Claude Code: Put a multi‑role AI squad in your repo

Sep 25, 2025
Vibe Sparking

Install Codex and BMAD, map BMAD’s “multi‑role experts” into AGENTS.md, then in the terminal say “*agent analyst”. Codex will run a BMAD‑style agile loop from requirements to delivery.

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

Two pain points in projects: no one writes clear requirements, and even when they do, no one lands them properly.

BMAD turns roles like Business Analysis, Architecture, Product, Development, QA, and Scrum into version‑controlled Markdown agents. Combined with Codex’s AGENTS.md, you work as if an AI squad is embedded in your repo.

This is a practical, copy‑ready path:

  1. Install BMAD
  2. Update AGENTS.md to register BMAD roles
  3. One prompt to bring in the Analyst and start

Why Codex + BMAD?

Section titled “Why Codex + BMAD?”
  • Codex understands AGENTS.md: it follows your project rules to build, check, and propose changes, in both CLI and IDE.
  • BMAD provides reusable “Agent as Code”: each role is a .md with tasks, checklists, templates, and workflows.
  • Together: “project rules + multi‑role collaboration + executable checks.” It feels like a real team that never forgets the process.

Step 1: Install BMAD (3‑minute quick path)

Section titled “Step 1: Install BMAD (3‑minute quick path)”

Ensure Node.js v20+ first.

Run in your project root:

This installs BMAD’s core structure and scripts into your project (including .bmad-core, templates, and helpers), preparing the agents for use.

Tip: If you see path errors, make sure directories include the leading dot (use .bmad-core, not bmad-core). This is a common gotcha.

Step 2: Register BMAD roles in AGENTS.md

Section titled “Step 2: Register BMAD roles in AGENTS.md”

AGENTS.md is a “README for agents.” It lives at the repo root, as standard Markdown. Use it to document your build/test/style/PR process and any local conventions.

Codex, Cursor, Gemini, and other agents read it; agents will follow rules you define here.

To help Codex quickly locate BMAD roles, add a small “BMAD Agents” section to AGENTS.md and list a clean mapping. Many teams prefer a YAML snippet for clarity and tooling, for example:

Note: AGENTS.md is Markdown and doesn’t require YAML—tables or lists are fine. The key is to let agents reliably locate those role .md files.

Step 3: Start the BMAD Analyst inside Codex

Section titled “Step 3: Start the BMAD Analyst inside Codex”

3.1 Launch Codex

Section titled “3.1 Launch Codex”

Install the CLI (choose one):

Then run codex in your project root and sign in with your ChatGPT account when prompted.

3.2 “One‑liner” to bring the Analyst online

Section titled “3.2 “One‑liner” to bring the Analyst online”

Once in Codex, say:

BMAD’s Web/IDE flows surface *help and *analyst as common entry points. Bringing the same “numbered options + task‑driven” style into Codex gives you a similar experience.

Reminder: BMAD’s Analyst/Architect/PM/Dev roles each define commands/checklists in agents/*.md. Some tasks (e.g., brainstorming) reference files in .bmad-core/tasks/*. If paths are correct, the agent can load them.

A “zero‑to‑one” cheat sheet (use as‑is)

Section titled “A “zero‑to‑one” cheat sheet (use as‑is)”

Setup

  • npx bmad-method install (or npm run install:bmad)
  • Verify .bmad-core exists and Node v20+ is installed

Configure

  • Document “how to build/test/code style/PR rules” in AGENTS.md
  • Add the “BMAD Agents” mapping so role files resolve correctly

Execute

  • Open codex and send the *agent analyst startup command
  • Produce docs/brief.md → Architect drafts architecture → Scrum Master shards into stories → Dev implements → QA validates

Make it faster: let Codex handle checks and PRs

Section titled “Make it faster: let Codex handle checks and PRs”

Codex naturally runs commands and reviews code in your repo (and can be @‑mentioned in PRs). When paired with AGENTS.md rules for tests/style/build, you get a tight loop of local validation and automated review.

Common pitfalls and fixes

Section titled “Common pitfalls and fixes”
  • Wrong path: missing the leading dot in .bmad-core causes load failures → standardize on dotted paths.
  • Scattered rules: put build/test/style/submit conventions in AGENTS.md so agents execute consistently.
  • Too many roles to remember: use *help in session or have the agent present numbered next steps.

Closing

Section titled “Closing”

BMAD turns human roles into versioned code. Codex turns execution into automatable actions. Wire them together and you get an AI team that doesn’t tire, reproduces well, and never forgets the rules.

Which project will you hand to this squad first? Share in the comments.

Quick interaction prompt

Section titled “Quick interaction prompt”

Which BMAD role would you customize deeply — Analyst, Architect, or QA?
Share your reason and one team rule that must always be enforced.

Tags:
  • BMAD
  • Claude Code
  • codex
  • Codex CLI
  • OpenAI
  • GPT-5
